title: Japan Visa Fees Jump 5x for Visa-Required Travelers
excerpt: Japan raised visa fees up to 400% on July 1, 2026, its first change since 1978. Here's who pays more, who's exempt, and how to budget for a Japan trip now.
content:
If you're planning a trip to Japan and need a visa to get in, budget more than you did last year. As of July 1, 2026, Japan raised its visa issuance fees for the first time since 1978, and the jump is not a modest inflation adjustment - it is roughly a fivefold increase across the board.

The new numbers are straightforward. A single-entry visa now costs 15,000 yen, up from 3,000 yen, and a multiple-entry visa costs 30,000 yen, up from 6,000 yen. In dollar terms that puts a single-entry visa at roughly 93 US dollars and a multiple-entry visa at around 186 dollars, translating to somewhere near 7,500 to 15,000 rupees, or 86 to 172 euros, depending on the exact exchange rate on the day you apply. Japan's Cabinet approved the revision on June 19, 2026, and it took effect less than two weeks later for any application submitted at an overseas diplomatic mission on or after July 1.

Who actually pays this? Only travelers from countries that require a visa to enter Japan in the first place. That list runs to more than 100 countries, including China, India, Vietnam, the Philippines, Indonesia, Bangladesh, Pakistan, and a long list of nations across Africa, the Middle East and Latin America. If your passport already lets you enter Japan visa-free - which covers roughly 70 or so countries and territories including the US, UK, Australia, Canada, New Zealand, the EU, Singapore, South Korea, Hong Kong and Malaysia - this fee change does not touch you directly, and your entry stays exactly as free and simple as before.

Japan's Foreign Minister, Toshimitsu Motegi, framed the increase around nearly five decades of currency and cost drift: a 3,000 yen fee set in 1978 simply doesn't cover what it costs to process a visa application and manage immigration today. Government officials have also pointed to the practical side effects they expect the extra revenue to fund, including language-support programs for Japan's growing foreign resident population (now above 4 million) and stronger enforcement against visa overstays. Officials have been careful to note that even after the increase, Japan's visa costs remain broadly in line with fees charged by other major destinations, and they don't expect the change to meaningfully dent inbound tourism given how strong demand has been.

There's a second cost travelers should know about, even though it's a separate line item from the visa fee. Japan's so-called Sayonara Tax - the departure tax folded into every airline or cruise ticket out of the country - is set to triple from 1,000 yen to 3,000 yen, with a tentative July 2026 timeline tied to the same fiscal year tax reform package, though this piece still needs final parliamentary sign-off in some reporting. Unlike the visa fee, the departure tax applies to essentially everyone leaving Japan by air or sea, regardless of nationality or visa status, with narrow exemptions for crew, short transit passengers and children under two.

The two changes land differently depending on your passport, but together they add a real, if modest, cost to a Japan trip. For a visa-required traveler on a single-entry tourist visa, you're now looking at roughly 12,000 yen more for the visa itself (about 75 dollars) plus an extra 2,000 yen (about 13 dollars) in departure tax once that change is finalized - not enough to derail a trip, but enough to build into your budget rather than discover at the visa counter.

So what should you actually do? If you're a citizen of a visa-required country - China, India, Vietnam, Indonesia and dozens of others - and you're planning Japan travel, budget the new 15,000 yen (or 30,000 yen for multiple-entry) fee into your trip cost now, since this isn't a proposal still working through parliament, it's already in effect for any application filed today. If you're applying through an agent or visa service rather than directly at a Japanese consulate, double-check that their published pricing reflects the post-July 1 fee rather than an outdated rate. If your passport is on Japan's visa-exempt list, nothing changes for your entry requirements, though it's worth knowing that Japan is separately developing an electronic travel authorization system called JESTA, expected around fiscal year 2028, that will eventually require even visa-exempt travelers to get a low-cost digital pre-approval before flying - not a near-term concern, but a trend worth watching if you visit Japan regularly. And regardless of your visa status, factor the departure tax change into your return-leg budget once it's confirmed, since it's collected automatically through your ticket rather than something you pay separately at the airport.
